coredata.dc:description: Due to the simplicity of fabricating the carbon dots (CQDs), which allows for scalability and cost-effectiveness, and their fascinating size-dependent physical properties, such functional materials are an enormous promise for many emergent technological applications. This has positioned these functional materials as highly promising candidates for a plethora of cutting-edge technological applications. Notable examples included solar cells, phototherapy, sensing, and environmental applications, which will be addressed in this review underscore their immense promise in emergent technologies. Here, we present a fuller understanding of the synthetic methods and physical properties of CQDs through the rationalization of many experimental and theoretical results. This includes discussions on their optical, electronic, and structural characteristics, elucidating the intricate interplay between these factors. The synthesis-structure-application triad is critically analyzed to draw connections between the fabrication methods, intrinsic properties, and functionality of CQDs. By highlighting the advancements and challenges in each of these domains, we aim to offer a comprehensive overview that can serve as a valuable resource for researchers and practitioners in the field. Finally, we hope that this review can, in principle, direct future experimental and theoretical research related to CQDs, in order to shed some light on the broad spectrum of CQDs applications a priori.

(b) Summary of the postsynthetic strategies usually used to modify the CQDs surface functional groups which leads to highly tunable physical properties [70]. (c) The “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods used for the prepare CQDs.

(a) Illustration of quantum confinement effect [146]. (b) UV–vis absorption and (c) normalized PL spectra of CQDs [169]. (d) Synthetic protocol for the prepare multicolor fluorescent CQDs and digital images of these samples under UV light (excited at 365 nm) [148]. (e) DFT calculations on the effect of chemical doping on the HOMO/LUMO energy levels of CQDs [170]. (f) Illustration of small crystalline domains in CQDs and the HOMO/LUMO energy gap as functions of the sp2-hybridization factor of carbon domains [171].

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the simplicity of fabricating the carbon dots (CQDs), which allows for scalability and cost-effectiveness, and their fascinating size-dependent physical properties, such functional materials are an enormous promise for many emergent technological applications. This has positioned these functional materials as highly promising candidates for a plethora of cutting-edge technological applications. Notable examples included solar cells, phototherapy, sensing, and environmental applications, which will be addressed in this review underscore their immense promise in emergent technologies. Here, we present a fuller understanding of the synthetic methods and physical properties of CQDs through the rationalization of many experimental and theoretical results. This includes discussions on their optical, electronic, and structural characteristics, elucidating the intricate interplay between these factors. The synthesis-structure-application triad is critically analyzed to draw connections between the fabrication methods, intrinsic properties, and functionality of CQDs. By highlighting the advancements and challenges in each of these domains, we aim to offer a comprehensive overview that can serve as a valuable resource for researchers and practitioners in the field. Finally, we hope that this review can, in principle, direct future experimental and theoretical research related to CQDs, in order to shed some light on the broad spectrum of CQDs applications a priori.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (denoted as CQDs) are known to be a new type of carbon-based nanoparticles usually prepared with dimensions in the range of 1–10 nm. It has been widely reported that the CQDs have a strong luminescence, especially in the visible region, low cytotoxicity, water dispersibility, biocompatibility, photochemical stability, and so on [1–20]. Since their discovery in 2004, mainly owing to their outstanding physical properties and simplicity of manufacture, these CQDs can be utilized in different conceivable applications, for example, such as sensors [21,22], optoelectronic devices [23,24], catalysis [25,26], antibacterial agents [27,28], energy storage materials [29,30], and so on [1–68]. From Fig. 1 (a) , it is possible to see the scientific community's interest in these CQDs for a broad spectrum of technological applications. In this review, we introduce the basic principle of the relation of CQDs-based materials synthesis, electronic structure as well as fundamental properties as a strategy to boost their use in solar cells, phototherapy, sensing, and environmental applications. Experimentally, the first CQDs observed were during the purification process of single-walled carbon nanotubes obtained from the arc discharge method [1]. After this discovery, developed numerous methods of syntheses to obtain CQDs. Thus, the developed methods seek a good cost-benefit ratio, dimension control as well as differentiated functions, structures and compositions.2 Also, in this way, the methods used to prepare of CQDs can usually be classified into two types: “top-down” and “bottom-up” (see Fig. 1 (c)). Particularly, it is well-known that the difference between these strategies is the precursors used in the syntheses. In the case of the “top-down” method, the CQDs are prepared from carbon precursors with macroscopic structures, such as diamond, graphite, activated carbon, carbon nanotubes and graphite oxide [2–6,11,21–23,69–79]. In the case of the "bottom-up" approach, the CQDs are synthesized from molecular precursors [80], for example, citric acid, glucose, sucrose and resin [36].

It is well-known that the surface engineering of CQDs materials, summary in Fig. 1 (b), can expand its technological use significantly, as recently demonstrated [81]. This involves reactions with a variety of the organic molecules, such as amines, carboxylic acids, thiols, and so on, allowing for the attachment of specific groups to enhance solubility, stability, as well as, their chemical reactivity. Also, it is well-known that replacing existing surface ligands with new ones in general allows precise control over the surface chemistry of these CQDs. This strategy also facilitates the encapsulation of CQDs. For instance, the encapsulating CQDs within a silica shell provides additional protection, improved stability, and enhanced biocompatibility. Silica-coated CQDs as a strategy to expand their applications in areas such as bioimaging and drug delivery [82,83]. Hence, in this direction, the post-synthetic strategies used for modifying CQDs surface functional groups are diverse and versatile. By employing these approaches, researchers can precisely tailor the physical and chemical properties of CQDs, enabling their customization for specific applications such as sensors, imaging agents, catalysis, drug delivery systems, and so on. At long last, we trust that the knowledge in-depth of such changes, in particular, may provide new chemical perspectives that allow elucidating their fundamental properties at the nanoscale, as well as, might be useful to leverage the emergence of new technologies. A thorough understanding of these CQDs-based material synthesis strategies will be reviewed in detail from the next section.

Therefore, through of this review, we are delighted to present a comprehensive discuss how an understanding of synthesis and their underlying properties can contribute to leverage the potential applications of these promising systems based on CQDs. The top-down and bottom-up synthesis approaches are discussed in detail, underscoring their significance in tailoring the properties of CQDs for specific applications. We believe that this approach based on a variety of theoretical and experimental studies related to CQDs is key to developing novel advanced nanoscale materials with entirely new and tailored properties for many technological applications of interest [84,85]. Particularly, the electronic structure properties of CQDs, in general, are of great interest for the comprehension of their physical properties [86–88]. Hence, the thorough exploration presented in this review seeks to consolidate knowledge, guide future research, and inspire the development of innovative technologies harnessing the unique attributes of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: An overview of the CQDs preparation strategies

ce:section.ce:para: Simplistically, an arc discharge can be described as the electrical decomposition of a gas to generate plasma. Technically, it involves vaporisation of graphite by means of an electric arc discharge generated between two electrodes [2,61,89,90]. The anode is filled with powdered carbon and the catalyst, while the cathode is composed of pure graphite, and both electrodes are placed in a chamber in an inert atmosphere [2,61]. The electrodes must be separated by a distance of less than 1 mm in order to establish an electrical current, and for that current to generate plasma. The temperature gradient can approach 4000 °C, which exceeds the sublimation temperature of carbon and causes carbon-material transfer. Consequently, the anode wears out and the inter-electrode distance needs to be adjusted to ensure that the plasma remains stable [2,61]. This technique, which is characterized by its simplicity and efficiency, widely employed for the synthesis of nanomaterials, particularly CQDs, offering the advantages of high water solubility, uniform size, and high quantum yield of the produced CQDs, demonstrating positive results for cell membrane and cytoplasm imaging [91].

Chao-Mujica et al. [14] used an underwater discharge process in water to prepare CQDs without any additional passivation procedure. Graphite electrodes with different rod diameters were used as the cathode and anode. Bubbles, which generally act as small reactors isolated by atmospheric water, form around the arc during this process [14], which is briefly described in the following way: the carbon nanostructure precursor is prepared in the first stage of the process, where steam and carbon are produced by sublimation. Graphite and graphene fragments then form by thermal exfoliation owing to the high plasma temperature; consequently, new bonds to carbon are created that lead to the formation of CQD structures by homogeneous nucleation (with carbon vapour alone) or the heterogeneous formation of polyhedral compounds, as well as large disordered polyhedral particles. Finally, the oxidation reactions involving these exfoliated fragments, which did not form fully closed carbon structures, and water molecules produce graphene oxide or passivate the CQDs [14]. This study determined whether or not the prepared CQDs can be used as potential fluorescent biomarkers in vitro. Indeed, positive results for the cell membrane and cytoplasm were observed by fluorescence microscopy after 2 h [14].

Baslak et al. [92] developed an innovative approach to synthesise CQDs using fermented tea as a natural material and carbon source, through the arc discharge method. The synthesized CQD particles, approximately 10 nm in size, were thoroughly characterized. Additionally, the potential application of these CQDs as electrolytes in capacitors was evaluated through a high-speed charge-discharge method [92]. Research is ongoing to refine the arc discharge method to achieve better control over CQD size, morphology, and properties. Modifications like using different electrode materials, varying the discharge parameters, and introducing additional reagents during synthesis have been explored to tailor the characteristics of the CQDs. While arc discharge-synthesized CQDs exhibit good fluorescence, their non-uniform particle size and low yield hinder scalability [93,94]. Despite its limitations, the arc discharge method remains a valuable tool for CQD synthesis, and research is ongoing to improve its control and scalability.

Likewise, laser ablation is a well-known method that is similar to arc discharge; in which a carbon target is irradiated with a laser beam [15,95]. The laser ablation method offers precise control over the synthesis process, resulting in the production of CQDs with strong fluorescence, tunable colour emission, easy surface functionalization, low cost, and high aqueous stability. Sufficient energy is available to remove electrons from the volume and penetrate the surface of the material. The process involves heating the solid target material to approximately 1200 °C, which causes the material to vaporise through a solid-to-plasma transition, with generated carbon nanoparticles swept along with a flowing inert gas; a laser-induced plasma plume forms through collisions between evaporated species (atoms and clusters) [2,15,16]. The plasma plume is extinguished as the pressure between the ambient atmosphere and the plasma-state system varies that, under certain temperature and pressure conditions, initiates nanoparticle nucleation and growth, with agglomeration finally occurring, forming larger structures. Bonds can form between the generated particles at contact points; these bonds significantly reflect the properties of the primary particles because laser generation provides a very clean surface [2,15,16], contributing to the purity and reproducibility of the synthesized CQDs.

Passivation CQD surfaces with simple organic species provides particles with various dimensions. In this case, large carbon particles (approximately 20 nm) with surfaces passivated in the same manner as small carbon particles (approximately 5 nm) were found to be less photoluminescent. In this case, the photoluminescence (PL) emissions occurs through the quantum confinement of emissive energy traps on CQD particle surfaces [18]. The laser pulse width interferes with the nucleation and growth conditions of CQDs of different dimensions. It is well-known that the shorter pulse widths generally lead to smaller and more uniform CQDs, while longer pulse widths may result in larger and more diverse particle sizes [19,96]. Thus, a higher supersaturation or cluster density in the bubble is beneficial in the nucleation and growth process [19]. In short, the laser ablation method stands out for its precision in controlling the synthesis parameters, leading to well-defined CQDs with tailored properties. Recent advancements in laser technology have enabled the development of high-throughput laser ablation systems, paving the way for scalable production of CQDs. Hence, Its precise control over synthesis parameters, coupled with the ability to produce high-quality CQDs with clean surfaces, makes it an attractive method for a wide range of desired applications, including optoelectronic devices, biomedical imaging, sensors, and so on [2,15–18].

Electrochemical synthesis generally involves the use of three electrodes (carbon precursor, auxiliary and reference) [9,97]. The CQDs produced through this method exhibit good electrocatalytic activity, high chemical stability, and good biocompatibility, making them promising candidates for various applications, including biosensors, drug delivery, and bioimaging [3,8–12,31–33]; This electrochemical synthesis method is well-known to provide high purity structures in high synthetic yields, is simple to manipulate, and very reproducibly provides new materials with particle size controlled by adjusting the applied potential [3,8–12,31–33]. As is well-known, the choice of electrolyte influences the reaction and properties of the resulting CQDs. In addition, the pH of the medium is known to be a critical factor in the electrochemical oxidation process [3,8–12,31–33]. The method is highly adaptable, allowing for the incorporation of different carbon sources and customization of reaction conditions. Depending on the intended applications, additional post-processing steps may be carried out. This can include surface functionalization, doping, or encapsulation to tailor the properties of the CQDs for specific uses.

This approach harnesses electrochemical reactions to fabricate CQDs from carbon-rich precursors. Common precursors include glucose, citric acid, or other carbon-containing molecules. Noteworthily, the group of Zhou [8] was the first to electrochemically synthesise CQDs using carbon-precursor electrodes in paper form, a platinum electrode, and Ag/AgClO4 as the reference. Oxidation was evidenced by the colourless electrolyte solution exhibiting blue luminescence when irradiated with an ultraviolet (UV) lamp [2,8,10,11]. This oxidative process leads to the formation of reactive intermediates generated at the electrode surface undergo further reactions, resulting in the formation of CQDs. The size and physical properties of the CQDs can, in principle, be influenced by controlling the electrochemical conditions such as voltage, current, and reaction time. However, despite some advantages, the need to purify the prepared CQDs is a significant disadvantage [2,8,10,11]. In addition, the prepared CQDs exhibit low PL quantum yields (PLQYs) of generally less than 10% [4–7,31,33]. Particularly, PLQY can be defined as a measure associated with the efficiency to convert absorbed light into emitted light [98,99].

This strategy usually involves measuring the PL to determine the quality of prepared CQD samples with differently sized particles. The PL emission spectra of CQDs prepared at various current densities were acquired, showing the relationship between particle size and integrated PL emission measured at current densities of about 180 mAcm−2; 100 mAcm−2; and 20 mAcm−2 [31].

CQDs produced with the abovementioned strategy were used to detect ferric ions (Fe3+) in potable water (faucet) [20]. The particles were found to be very dispersible and exhibited bright blue luminescence when irradiated with UV light at 365 nm. The material showed good selectivity, precision, and reproducibility in real samples when used in a ferric-ion assay. The presented results were explained using FTIR spectroscopy, which exhibited an absorption at approximately 1634 cm−1 that corresponds to CO stretching vibrations in the original CQDs [20]. However, this peak was not observed in the spectrum of the precipitate formed after the addition of Fe3+, thereby providing direct evidence for carboxyl/Fe3+ interactions [20].

Chemical oxidation methods generally treat various carbon precursors with strong oxidisers [2,9,34,100]. This is a versatile strategy for the large-scale production of CQDs [101–103]. In particular, CQDs obtained by chemical oxidation usually exhibit similar particle-size distributions, compositions, PL properties, and surface properties, irrespective of the type of precursor used to synthesise them [2,9,34]. Strong oxidizing agents, often acidic in nature, are introduced to initiate the chemical oxidation process. Common oxidizers include nitric acid, sulfuric acid, or other potent chemicals capable of promoting the oxidative conversion of the carbon precursor. The reaction conditions, including temperature, reaction time, and concentrations of the oxidizing agent, are carefully controlled to influence the size, morphology, and surface functionalization of the synthesized CQDs [2,9,34,100]. The consistent outcomes achieved through chemical oxidation make it a robust strategy for tailoring CQDs to meet the demands of various technological applications.

Syntheses using various precursors, including petroleum coke [35], carbon-rich flour [36] and activated carbon [37], produced CQDs with similar diameters (5–8 nm on average), as well as uniform dispersions [36]. Consequently, a blue-green-red spectral composition with a yield of up to 48% and a PLQY of approximately 9.2% was obtained for flour rich in carbon, while CQDs prepared from activated charcoal exhibited a blue-green bloom with a PLQY of 16.2% that increased to 26% when doped with ZnO [37]. Additionally, the chemical oxidation method is known for its potential to provide crystalline CQDs. Hence, chemical oxidation methods can easily be scaled up for large-scale production, making them suitable for industrial applications in areas such as sensors, imaging, and optoelectronics.

The sonochemical method is environmentally friendly, mainly because it does not use harmful or toxic substances; this strategy is, of course, based on the application of high-power ultrasonic waves to boost the chemical reaction of the desired mixture [104,105]. In principle, high-power ultrasonic waves produce alternating low- and high-pressure waves in a liquid, which results in the formation, as well as the collapse of small vacuum bubbles in a physical process referred to as “cavitation” [38,39]. Thus, these “cavitations” lead to high-velocity impacts, liquid jets, and deagglomeration; hence strong shearing is produced through hydrodynamic forces [2,40–42]. The intense cavitation-induced forces facilitate the breaking down of the carbon-rich precursor into nanoscale fragments, leading to the formation of CQDs. Consequently, ultrasonic energy can be used to cut macroscopic carbon materials to scales below 10 nm (i.e., CQDs). Furthermore, this strategy enables CQDs to be easily prepared through nucleation, polymerisation, and/or aromatisation [2,38,106]. In essence, the sonochemical method not only champions environmental sustainability by avoiding the use of harmful substances but also harnesses the power of ultrasonic waves to induce controlled and intricate processes leading to the creation of CQDs.

Indeed, the main advantages of this strategy include one-step low-cost CQDs synthesis, fast reaction rates, and high yields, which result in uniformly small particles [10]. In addition, this strategy has been widely used to doping CQDs with bulk metals (e.g., Ga, In, Bi, Sn, Pb, Cd, Sb, Zn and so on) [38,107]. Kumar et al. [108] reported the formation of Sn@C-dots and a hybrid Sn@C-dots@Sn anode for lithium-ion batteries using this approach.

CQDs present in the liquid phase combine with oxygen free radicals to form oxidised CQDs. The incorporated oxygen atoms are stable and uniformly produced in this process, that is, they are present on the surfaces of the CQDs even after high-temperature activation, which usually significantly improves their chemical and thermal properties [43]. Also, the sonochemically prepared CQDs may undergo purification steps to remove any residual precursors or by-products. Common purification methods include centrifugation, dialysis, or column chromatography. Therefore, from this perspective, the unique combination of cavitation-induced effects allows for the tailored synthesis of CQDs, making this method a promising and eco-friendly avenue for the scalable production of nanomaterials with diverse applications.

A polyamide oligomer resin was used as the carbon precursor to prepare white fluorescent CQDs that are well dispersed but poorly crystalline [45]. As a result, these CQDs exhibited a PLQY of 3.3%; which increased to 28.3% when a co-passivating agent was applied. This methodology has been demonstrated to yield N-doped CQDs from graphite rods and ammonium hydroxide, leading to the formation of new energy levels in the bandgap, and facilitating physical-property modulation the prepared CQDs are promising probes for the detection of cobalt (II) ions and nucleic acids [44].

Finally, the sonochemical synthesis of CQDs is a promising approach with significant advantages in terms of environmental friendliness, efficiency, controllability, and versatility [107,109]. As research continues to unravel the underlying mechanisms and address the challenges, the sonochemical method is expected to play a crucial role in the development of next-generation CQDs with tailored properties for a wide range of applications.

Microwave have been widely used as a quick, clean, and low-cost technique for the preparation of CQDs [20,31,110]. Since microwaves strongly interact with carbon-based materials, this emerging strategy facilitates fast, uniform heating [20,31]. In addition, heating occurs owing to alternating electric and magnetic fields that interact with the dipole moments of polar molecules present in a solvent, which promotes constant and consistent volumetric heating [38,48,111]. Various biomasses can be used to prepare CQDs using this strategy, and the microwave method is usually used to effectively shorten reaction times and provide homogeneous heating, which can lead to uniform CQD particle-size distributions [2,49–52].

Hagiwara et al. [112] reported synthesis and characterization of monodispersed CQDs by microwave route. Importantly, the above-mentioned characteristics are critical to many CQD-based technologies. Finally, the basic principles of the physical mechanism associated with the formation of monodispersed nanocrystals have been elucidated in the literature for a large variety of organic and inorganic compounds [50–52,112–115], however, this remains an open field for further investigation. In another study, Mexican mint leaves (Plectranthus amboinicus) were used as the carbon precursor [54]. As a result, the CQDs prepared by microwave synthesis from this precursor showed a blue emission (with a PLQY of approximately 17%), and a spherical-like morphology with a mean diameter of about 2.43 ± 0.02 nm. Also, the oxygen-containing functional groups on their surfaces result in water solubility; consequently, the prepared CQDs were used as fluorescent sensors to detect Fe3+ with excellent sensitivity and selectivity exhibited [54].

These studies collectively emphasize the potential of microwave-assisted methods for the rapid and efficient synthesis of CQDs, opening up opportunities for various applications, including metal ion detection, adsorption, and fluorescence-based technologies. The method is particularly advantageous for large-scale production due to its quick reaction times. Additionally, microwave synthesis is a greener approach as it often requires lower reaction temperatures and reduces the need for excess solvents. Nevertheless, the growing interest in this technique underscores its potential as a powerful tool for the CQD synthesis with tailored properties for a wide range of technological applications.

Thermal decomposition is based on pyrolysis or carbonisation process of carbon precursors, with associated thermal reactions that are typically endothermic [2,55–57,116]. This process typically takes place in a furnace or an oven. The decomposition is carried out under specific temperature conditions to facilitate the breakdown of the precursor into carbon fragments. These intermediates subsequently undergo further reactions, such as polymerisation and aromatisation, resulting in the formation of CQDs. After the desired reaction time and temperature, in general, cool the reaction mixture to quench the synthesis process. This step helps stabilize and prevent further growth of the CQDs. The advantages of this method include the use solvent-free approaches, wider precursor tolerance, less take-up time, cost-effectiveness, and scalability [58–61]. The method is suitable for large-scale production and can be tailored to achieve specific CQD properties by adjusting the synthesis parameters.

Biocompatible CQDs were obtained by the thermal decomposition of citric acid and various biogenic amines (e.g., histamine, putrescine, cadaverine, and spermine) [62]. The stability of each precursor was observed to decrease it reacted and amides formed on the CQD surface, which mainly ascribable to carboxyl/amino crosslinking. However, these CQDs have zero net charge and, consequently, are highly stable in environments with different pH values because their neutrality precludes ionic interactions with charged components in the bacterial membrane [62].

Hexagonally patterned monodispersed CQDs were prepared from fennel seeds (Foeniculum vulgare); product exhibited excellent colloidal solubility, significant photostability, and was also stable at different pHs values without the need for surface passivation [63]. The synthesized CQDs exhibited a PLQY of approximately 9.5% [63]. These results highlight the possible use of these CQDs in biosensing, cell-imaging, solar-cell, and sensor applications.

The hydro(solvo)thermal method is a well-established technique for the large-scale production of CQDs with high quantum yields [2,38,56,61,64,65,117]. From this approach uses a wide temperature range and a high vapour pressure, which enables the morphology, particle size, as well as the composition of synthesized CQDs to be precisely controlled. Environmental friendliness, cost-effectiveness, and a lack of toxicity are advantages of the hydrothermal or solvothermal strategy as an approach for producing new carbon-based materials from various precursors [2,38,56,61,64,65]. This versatile and scalable method offers controlled conditions for CQD formation with entirely new properties.

Jamaludin et al. [66] reported the extraction of CQDs from empty fruit peels as a carbon biochar precursor. This hydrothermal extraction process does not involve aggressive chemicals; rather, the combination of pressure and temperature acting on the precursor leads to the formation of uniform CQDs. The prepared material exhibited optical properties that depended on the excitation wavelength. Thus, the presence of carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl functional groups led to water solubility and negatively charged surface, and the prepared material exhibited a PLQY of 55.84%, a morphology with particles approximately 3 nm in size, and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm [66].

Pandiyan et al. [67] reported a green method for the synthesis of CQDs from sugarcane as the precursor. The CQDs were found to be highly fluorescent, exhibiting sky-blue fluorescence when irradiated with long-wavelength ultraviolet light (365 nm) and a PLQY of approximately 17.98%. These as-prepared spherical CQDs are monodispersed with particles 0.75–2.75 nm in size, (mean: 1.7 ± 0.2 nm). In addition, these CQDs were structurally characterised, which confirmed the amorphous nature of the graphitic carbon as well as the presence of hydrophilic surface groups (e.g., –OH, –COOH, and –NH2) that endow the CQDs with high water solubility [67].

Yang et al. [68] used the hydrothermal method to prepare CQDs with double PL emission, with various precursors, such as o-phenylenediamine, m-phenylenediamine, and p-phenylenediamine, and various process temperatures in the 140–200 °C range used. Likewise, these researchers also studied the effect of pH of the medium and concluded that the yellow emission is inhibited while the blue emission is promoted with increasing precursor-solution pH. The prepared CQDs were monodispersed with very homogeneous size distributions and almost spherical morphologies, with mean diameters of approximately 1.6, 1.5, and 3 nm obtained when o-phenylenediamine, m-phenylenediamine, and p-phenylenediamine were used as precursors, respectively [68].

In a similar manner, Hasan et al. [69] studied the influence of the precursor (i.e., hydroxymethylfurfural, furfural, and microcrystalline cellulose) during the preparation of CQDs, and revealed mean particle sizes of 6.36 ± 0.54 (microcrystalline cellulose), 5.35 ± 0.56 (hydroxymethylfurfural) and 3.94 ± 0.60 nm (furfural). In addition, these CQDs exhibited similar green PL emission trends with comparable intensities.

Fluorescent impurities can also form during the CQDs synthesis [118–120]. With this in mind, the impact of these impurities on the stabilities of the photophysical properties of CQDs prepared using the solvothermal method [118]. These authors showed that solvothermal reaction time can be used to experimentally control particle size. In additional, lower CQD PL emission and larger particles were reported with increasing time [118], which is consistent with literature reports for this material [31]. These studies showcase the versatility and tunability of hydro(solvo)thermal methods for CQD synthesis, highlighting their potential for eco-friendly production and customization of optical properties for various applications of interest.

The template method uses two main synthesis stages to prepare CQDs. In the initial stage, a template or scaffold is formed. This template can be a variety of materials, including polymers, dendrimers, or other nanostructures. The choice of template influences the final characteristics of the CQDs. First, calcination is performed in a mesoporous mould, after which the material is subjected to a corrosion process that removes the support and generates CQDs [55]. Spherical mesoporous silica is the most common template used in this technique; this material is noteworthy because it has a high surface area, large pore volume, as well as uniform, well distributed, and controllable pore sizes [118,121,122]. This strategy was used by Zong et al. [72] to synthesise ∼1.3-μm spheres with an average pore size of 3.60 nm starting from mesoporous silica spheres prepared with N-hexadecylamine as the surfactant, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) as the precursor, and ammonia as the catalyst. The spheres were subsequently soaked in a solution of complex salts and citric acid. Calcination and removal of the mesoporous silica support led to the formation of CQDs [72]. A similar route was used by Liu et al. [70] during the development of their chemical synthesis method, in which CQDs were prepared by calcining silica spheres followed by acid treatment. The obtained multi-coloured photoluminescent CQDs were used as benign “nano-lanterns”, which are highly desirable in bioimaging, disease-detection, and drug-delivery applications.

However, studies have been carried out with other moulds, either eliminated or not, after the formation of CQDs. Xiong et al. [71] synthesized CQDs at the liquid-air interface in model amphiphilic cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs), which were selected for the nucleation and direct growth of CQDs. Due to preferential interactions, the CNCs were densely coated with CQDs to form a decorated morphology devoid of excessive localised aggregation. The CQD morphology was described in terms of needle shape. An orthogonal orientation was observed during growth, which is ascribable to surface-energy anisotropy at the solid–liquid interface. Crystallinity similarity to that of graphene (2θ = 10.5°). These researchers observed different XRD diffraction data for the CQD crystals and aggregates, indicative of the presence of different nanostructures, which rationalises the observed changes in PL emission response [71]. The light emitted by the CQD crystals (542 nm) upon excitation by a light source is of higher energy than that of the initial CQD dispersion (565 nm), which is rationalised by the ordered CQD organisation in the crystal. Molecular rotation is inhibited by monolayer attachment to various facets, which restricts intermolecular energy transfer and facilitates constant photon emission [71].

The template method offers a level of precision and control over the CQD synthesis process, allowing for the creation of nanoscale structures with tailored properties. The choice of template, precursor, and carbonisation conditions enables researchers to fine-tune the characteristics of the CQDs for specific applications, such as sensors, imaging, and optoelectronics.

Micro-plasma synthesis is based on directly interacting plasma species with a precursor solution, in which molecules transform into structures of interest [123]. In particular, micro-plasma is generated in flowing argon, helium, or nitrogen when a high-voltage direct current is applied [74,76]. This method is well known to involve transformation stages that start with the production of chain intermediates that later polymerise and aggregate, followed by carbonisation or aromatisation. The final stage involves passivation, where the particle surface is modified through numerous functional groups [124]. Hence, complex reactions that can excite, ionise, or dissociate water and precursor compounds occur at the plasma–liquid interface [124]. The synthesized CQDs using the micro-plasma method have been reported to exhibit high crystallinity and photoluminescence properties, making them suitable for various applications.

For instance, CQDs that exhibit an intense blue PL emission were obtained using the microplasma-assisted method, which is ascribable to the influence of ions/electrons from the microplasma [78]. This study suggests that the electrons generated by the plasma, which can produce ions, are responsible for the observed higher luminescence. CQD surface functionalization occurs in a liquid medium through reactions involving species present in solution. Consequently, the microplasma promotes efficient particle nucleation through radical polymerisation.

Most CQDs reported in the literature exhibit a single emission peak when excited. However, CQDs with switchable characteristics (470 nm: blue; formed using a citric-acid/urea precursor solution, and 515 nm: green/blue; generated from pyrenetetrasulfonic acid tetrasodium salt hydrate) were prepared by Weerasinghe et al. [124] using atmospheric air to generate cold plasma (<65 °C). The concentration dependence of the CQD emission revealed that the switchable mechanism is likely ascribable to the formation of excimers of the 1,3,6,8-pyrenetetrasulfonic acid salt hydrate used in the synthesis, together with the cold atmospheric plasma [124].

Several characteristics are sought when manufacturing CQDs and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), including size control. Normally, these materials are prepared under steady-state conditions, with the residence time of the material in the reactor adjusted to deliver the required particle size with minimal dispersion and agglomeration [77]. Consequently, a low-cost process for the synthesis of GQDs using a thermal plasma jet was developed [75]. Ethylene gas, as the carbon source, was injected directly into the air plasma to generate a carbon beam that was directed to the anode (coupled to carbon tubes) and dispersed in the chamber. The study concluded that particle size is directly related to the size of the anode-coupled tubes. Because collisions occur during the passage of the ethylene gas through the carbon tube, more collisions occur in larger carbon tubes.

Tung et al. [74] synthesized CQDs using atmospheric-pressure microplasma, with a sucrose precursor solution used to prepare quantum carbon particles. A tungsten-rod anode and a graphite-rod cathode submerged in the precursor solution was used to generate the plasma. As a result, a spherical morphology was obtained, with approximately 99% of the particles having diameters of approximately 3 nm. A CQD solution emitted intense blue light when irradiated at 405 nm with a light-emitting diode (LED) [74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Progress in theoretical and experimental approaches for a fuller understanding of their physical properties and application based on CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The combination of theoretical insights and experimental results leads to a consolidated understanding of CQDs. This knowledge contributes to the development of guidelines for designing CQDs with specific attributes tailored for different applications. Usually, the CQDs structure exhibit a sphere-like shape, which is associated with that of the small, surface-passivated discs of graphene (i.e., a carbon core). They may also contain impurities such as oxygen, sulfur, and nitrogen surface groups depending on the precursor used in their synthesis [125–127]. The carbon core is predominantly composed of sp2-hybridised carbon atoms, along with a few s-triazine structures, benzyl units, and aliphatic groups, which are linked by C–N bonds [128–132]. Therefore, studying the unique physical and chemical properties of CQDs is important as it opens a wide range of possibilities in areas such as drug delivery, medical imaging, sensing devices, optoelectronic devices; thus, new applications and methodologies could emerge in order to utilize the physical properties of CQDs a priori.

In addition, the experimental findings obtained from TEM images reveal that the CQD structure is composed of two parts: the first part is usually formed by amorphous carbon cores (with sp3-hybridised carbon atoms) and the second part is formed owing to the partially hybridised sp2 carbon domains located on the CQD surface [192]. Thus, the CQD structure is composed of sp3-hybridised amorphous carbon with small sp2-hybridised atomic domains of various geometries (Fig. 2 (f)) [171]. In principle, the multicolor emission of CQDs can be explained by the partially hybridised sp2 carbon domains of their sp3-hybridised amorphous cores [171]. A red-shift in the PL emission of the CQDs with an increasing degree of surface oxidation has been widely reported [193–196]. Hence, the size of the particles and defective surface states influence the PL emission mechanism of the CQDs [197]. Typically, the differences observed in the absorption/emission spectra of these materials are closely related to their structures, compositions, and particle sizes [157].

Likewise, the crystalline nature of CQDs has extensively been investigated. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) results reveal that the broad reflections near 2θ = 20° are due to the low crystallinity of the graphitic structure [198]. Dhandapani et al. [199] reported CQDs synthesized from food waste, and a simple magnetic stirring XRD spectrum showed an amorphous crystalline structure with a broad diffraction peak of the (002) plane at 2θ = 24,02°. Surendran et al. [200] prepared CQDs via hydrothermal synthesis using honey, garlic, and ammonia, as carbon, sulfur, and nitrogen sources, respectively. As a result, the XRD patterns of all the prepared CQDs showed some similar crystallinities with a peak at 2θ = 23,00° corresponding to the (002) plane [200]. Raman spectroscopy is frequently used to determine the structure and all the sp2 carbon states [201]. From the literature, there are two dominant peaks in the Raman spectra of CQDs; one can be found near 1380 cm−1 (denoted as the D band) and the other can be found on a shoulder band near 1590 cm−1 (denoted as the G band) [202,203]. The D band is frequently associated with a disordered carbon structure originating from the vibrations in the sp3-hybridised carbon atoms. The G band denotes the existence of organised structures; the crystalline structures of the graphite lattice result from the vibrations of the sp2-bonded carbon atoms. Hence, the presence of the D and G bands confirms the successful construction of CQDs with the sp2 core and sp3-hybridised matrix [202,203]. Furthermore, a broadening of the D band is observed for samples of doped CQDs, suggesting an increase in the short-range structural disorder in the CQDs [203].

Understanding the solvent effect on the physical properties of the CQDs is critical for achieving better performance in the applications of interest. To this end, Paloncýová et al. [204] presented a full procedure for performing molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of CQDs with different particle sizes (1.3–5.7 nm in diameter) and various oxygen-containing surface functional groups in solvents such as water and dimethylformamide (DMF). A destabilising effect was observed in the presence of excess carbonyl groups or negatively charged carboxyl groups in water. The authors also investigated the intraparticle dynamics and demonstrated that the individual layers of CQDs rotate with each other. However, the layer rotation rate tended to decrease as the size of the layers and the number of functional groups increase, especially for those forming hydrogen bonds. Finally, they studied the stabilisation effects of interlayer hydrogen bonds in the aprotic solvent, DMF. Interestingly, in this solvent, the absence of hydrogen bonding was balanced by the presence of an excess of interlayer hydrogen bonds. This explained the preference for aggregation in DMF, which could affect the other properties of CQDs [204]. Moreover, the presence of electron−hole states was revealed by light-induced electron paramagnetic resonance measurements. This indicated the susceptibility of the intersystem crossing of the trap state to the solvent polarity and hydrogen-bonding interactions between the shell and the solvent, which, in principle, can contribute to a better understanding of the PL quenching behaviour of the CQDs [139].

The scientific community is interested in exploring the properties of fluorescent CQDs for emergent medical applications [205–209]. In addition, it is important to highlight that these CQDs can easily be produced using various sustainable synthetic methods that medicinal plants are the starting materials, which can, in principle, lead to the potentiation of their medical effects [207,210]. In this line, Chen et al. [211] prepared small CQDs with a diameter of approximately 2.2 nm and a PLQY of 80% for bioimaging (a well-known non-invasive process for the visualisation of the biological activity). They reported that MCF-7 (a human breast cancer cell line) cells interacted well with the CQDs and lysosomes (commercial Lyso-Tracker Red), indicating that the efficiency of the CQDs is comparable to that of a commercial Lyso-Tracker [211]. On the other hand, the ability of CQDs to detect metal ions in cells provides the opportunity to create mechanisms of control for metal ion disorders in the human body. Some existing techniques can detect metal ions in specific samples, but most of those techniques are time-consuming, expensive, and require professionals to operate the equipment to limit the control process. Comparatively, optical methods as colorimetric and fluorometric dual readouts are sensitive, inexpensive and easy to operate [212–216].

As is well-known, iron (Fe) is one of the most abundant metals on Earth, and plays an important role in physiological processes, including metabolism and several other relevant cell functions [217–221]. By incorporating CQDs in colorimetry and fluorometric methods, it is possible to detect low amounts of Fe3+ in water samples and its presence in intracellular bioimaging. Shi et al. [222] reported the potential application of hybrid CQDs with particle dimensions between 5 and 8 nm for the detection of Fe3+ in bioimaging and biosensing. The prepared hybrid CQDs also exhibited a high PLQY of approximately 46.8%; the limit of detection was 52 nmol/L for Fe3+ ions [222]. Similar results were reported by Atchudan et al. [223], who synthesized CQDs by a hydrothermal method using the fruit Malus floribunda in an ammonia solution to incorporate nitrogen groups on the surface of the N-CQDs. In practice, the sensitivity of CQD-based sensors is strongly dependent on the PLQY values, which in turn depend on the synthesis method used [153]. A comparison between PLQYs and Fe3+ detection limit obtained for synthesized CQDs using different preparation methods and starting materials is summarized in Table 2 .

Likewise, the presence of heavy metals in water represents a concern for human health and ecosystems, and is often associated with many diseases [229–232]. Developing methodologies that allow detection with low fabrication and operational costs can be the gateway to improving water quality. From this perspective, CQDs have been used in ultra-sensitive environmental monitoring systems for the fast detection of heavy metals owing to their favourable characteristics and properties [233]. For instance, Tan et al. [151] reported N-doped CQDs with a diameter of approximately 3 ± 0.08 nm produced from a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis, with high PLQYs of up to 92.1% and excellent capacity to detect Hg2+; the limit was approximately 24.1 nM. Qiao et al. [234] developed a ternary composite based on CQD/polyethyleneimine(PEI)/SiO2 for the detecting of Cu2+, where the detection limit was reported to be 0.07 nM. Further, Liu et al. [235] produced CQDs from bamboo leaves (Sinarundinaria Nakai) with a dual emission at approximately 653 nm and 493 nm. Particularly, the dual emission of these CQDs showed promise for the detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ at low concentrations, with a detection limits of approximately 0.14 nM and 0.22 nM for Pb2+ and Hg2+, respectively [235]. Numerous studies based on CQDs for this application have been reported, with detection limits in the ranges of 2.3 nM–32 pM for arsenic [236,237], 32.5 nM–834 μM for cadmium [238,239], 3.4 nM–3.47 for copper [240,241], 0.59 nM–4.6 nM for lead [242,243], and 10 nM–5.5 nM for mercury [244,245]. Furthermore, these detection limits are well below the values recommended by the World Health Organisation, highlighting the potential of CQD-based sensors for the environmental monitoring of heavy metals [246].

While CQDs have wide possibilities in bioimaging, biosensing is another potential application-oriented in treating diseases [247–249]. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a non-invasive process that consists of a systematic or local application of a photosensitive compound (photosensitizer) which accumulates on a pathological tissue [137,250]. The initiation, activation and destruction of inappropriate cells are conducted by the absorption of light in a specific wavelength by the photosensitizer. These treatments could be used to deal with some chronic inflammations, alternative for drug-resistant, bacterial infections, and anticancer applications [250–252]. Usually, the PDT mechanisms involve two types of reactions. Firstly, in the triplet state of the photosensitizer, energy transfer occurs to the surrounding environment. This transfer prompts the release of hydrogen electrons from the photosensitizer, initiating hence the formation of free radicals and anion radicals. These radicals, in turn, interact with ambient oxygen, leads the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), renowned for their potent cellular effects, serve as key mediators in the destruction of target cells [250]. The second reaction pathway involves the transition triplet state of the photosensitizer in exited singlet state. Here, the oxygen molecules directly receive the energy from the exited photosensitizer, leading to the production of singlet oxygen. This highly reactive form of oxygen plays a pivotal role in inducing cellular damage and eventual cell demise [253–257]. Hence, researchers are delving into the versatility of CQDs in generating singlet oxygen for PDT applications. With attributes such as exceptional biocompatibility, water dispersion, non-toxicity, cost-effectiveness, and facile functionalization, CQDs as the next generation of photosensitizers present a tantalizing prospect for advancing PDT technology [258–260].

Furthermore, CQD-based materials are promising as nanodrug carriers for use in chemotherapy. Computational tools are widely used to analyse their potential for medical applications. In this context, Wolski [261] reported the use of in silico methods to understand the mechanism of interaction between doxorubicin (DOX) molecules on the CQD surface at different pH levels (7.4 and 5), followed by binding free energy calculations with directed MD. The CQD/DOX complex was more stable at pH 7.4, as the oxygen-containing groups on the side walls of the CQDs did not deprotonate and were conducive to strong intermolecular interactions with the drug. As a result, the CQD/DOX complex primarily interacted via electrostatic forces and hydrogen bonding. At an acidic pH, the DOX molecules bound to the carrier mainly through the hydrophobic part, which in turn decreased hydrogen bond-type interactions. In addition, the interaction strength of the complex increased with the coverage of the CQD surface, while the pH was a critical factor affecting the loading and the release of DOX on the carrier surface [261]. Concurrently, it was experimentally shown that the CQD/DOX systems used for drug delivery to A549 cells exhibited a pH-controlled release [262]. Further, fluorescence microscopy revealed that CQD/DOX was internalised into the A549 cells and localised mainly in the cytoplasm, releasing DOX into the nucleus [262]. Moreover, from an experimental point of view, drug release results in increased CQD emission. Hence, this feature of CQDs can be monitored over time [207,263,264].

Additionally, the CQDs are emerging as promising non-viral vectors for gene delivery, offering advantages such as biocompatibility, low toxicity, and the ability to overcome limitations associated with viral vectors. Recent studies have demonstrated their potential in delivering various genetic materials, including DNA, RNA, and vaccines [265–271]. As we know, the CQDs can easily be functionalized with positively charged groups to interact with negatively charged DNA molecules, forming stable CQD-DNA complexes [93,272,273]. In a 2012 study, Liu et al. [274] utilized a microwave-assisted pyrolysis technique to synthesise CQDs functionalized with branched polyethylenimine (PEI). The presence of PEI on the CQD surface resulted in both increased fluorescence intensity and enhanced DNA condensation through electrostatic interactions. Furthermore, these PEI-functionalized CQDs demonstrated improved gene delivery capabilities in HepG2 cells, as evidenced by higher levels of gene expression compared to the use of PEI alone [274]. Zhou et al. [275] successfully used alginate-derived CQDs as carriers for plasmid TGF-b1 (pTGF-b1) delivery into 3T6 cells. While Chen et al. [276] investigated the potential of CQDs derived from porphyra polysaccharide and coated with ethylenediamine (EDA) as a gene delivery system for neuronal differentiation of ectodermal mesenchymal stem cells. Hence, these CQDs were loaded with the transcription factors Ascl1 and Brn2, and the resulting nanoparticle complex (CQDs/pDNA nanoparticles) exhibited superior neuronal differentiation efficiency compared to traditional induction methods. Similar to DNA delivery, CQDs can be used to carry various types of RNA, including messenger RNA (mRNA), small interfering RNA (siRNA), and microRNA (miRNA) [277–281]. Hence, this opens up possibilities for gene silencing therapies, gene editing, and protein expression modulation. The CQDs synthesized using citric acid and pentaethylenehexamine (PEHA) have shown high transfection efficiency and low cytotoxicity, effectively binding and delivering DNA and RNA into cells [270]. Also, CQDs have demonstrated the ability to cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB), a significant obstacle in gene therapy for neurological disorders [270,282,283]. In another study, CQDs functionalized with S-nitroso-N-acetylpenicillamine (SNAP) have been proposed as a potential aerosolized treatment for respiratory infections, including those caused by coronaviruses (HCoV). This highlights the versatility of CQDs in delivering therapeutics to the respiratory system. Furthermore, CQDs have shown promise as intranasal vaccines, particularly in the context of SARS-CoV-2 [284]. These vaccines have been shown to induce robust mucosal immunity and broad-spectrum neutralization against various SARS-CoV-2 variants, including the highly transmissible Omicron variant. This highlights the potential of CQDs in developing effective vaccines against rapidly evolving viruses. The versatility of CQDs as gene delivery platforms is further underscored by their ability to transfect hard-to-reach cells and serve as robust carriers for diverse genetic materials. These findings underscore the growing potential of CQDs in gene therapy, drug delivery, and vaccine development [285]. However, further research is needed to optimize the design and functionalization of CQDs for specific gene delivery applications. Additionally, addressing concerns regarding their long-term stability, biodistribution, and potential immunogenicity is crucial for their clinical translation. Despite these challenges, the promising results obtained so far highlight the significant potential of CQDs as a next-generation gene delivery platform with the ability to revolutionize the treatment of genetic disorders, infectious diseases, and cancer vaccines [265–271].

Nie et al. [286] developed an interesting antibacterial composite based on the addition of CQDs (0 wt% to 5 wt%) in the polyacrylonitrile (PAN)/DMF solution electrospun fibre. As a result, these composites presented low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, as well as resulting in an excellent inactivation agent for Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Bacillus subtilis with values up to 99.99% of inactivation upon illumination with visible light (Xe lamp, 500 W, 12 cm sample distance, 420 nm, 1.5 h) [286]. Li et al. [287] reported the successful synthesis of low-toxic and degradable CQDs exhibiting potent broad-spectrum antibacterial and antifungal properties. In this study, the computational models were based on the dimer of vitamin C, the precursor used in the electrochemical synthesis of the CQDs. From this perspective, the MD simulations revealed the CQDs' ability to destabilize DNA structures, i.e., causing significant distortion in the base pair binding mode, particularly at the terminal position. Notably, the theoretical simulations demonstrated the strengthening of intermolecular interactions between the CQDs and the DNA hairpin over time, shedding light on the origin of the nanomaterial's antibacterial activity [287].

Research on creating green technologies, such as light emitting diodes (LEDs) and solar cells, opens new perspectives for using CQDs-based materials [227–246,288–293]. In LEDs, it is well-known that the rapid and efficient radiative recombination of excitons is crucial for high light output and energy efficiency. In contrast, photovoltaic devices like solar cells rely on the effective separation of excitons into free charge carriers (electrons and holes) to generate electricity. This fundamental difference in the desired fate of excitons highlights the distinct design principles and material requirements for these two classes of optoelectronic devices [294]. Wang et al. pioneered the use of CQDs as the emissive layer in electroluminescent white LEDs (WLEDs) in 2011, marking a significant advancement in CQD-based LED technology [295]. This breakthrough was followed by Chen et al., 2012, who further expanded the applications of CQDs by demonstrating their use as phosphors in both monochromatic- and W-LEDs [296]. Additionally, researchers have successfully fabricated monochrome electroluminescent LEDs across the blue-to-red spectrum, utilizing the stable fluorescence emission of CQDs [297]. The progress in this field has been extensively reviewed by Ji et al. [298], highlighting the potential of CQDs as a versatile material for next-generation lighting devices.

Additionally, from a synthetic point of view, the CQDs produced with broad and narrow emission spectra play pivotal roles in the manufacture of desired optoelectronic devices, distinguished primarily by their emission profiles [299]. Broad emissive CQDs, characterized by a wider particle size distribution, exhibit a broad emission spectrum due to their diverse energy levels [300]. They find utility in display technologies for achieving vivid colors, in lighting applications demanding a wide spectral rendering, and in multi-analyte sensors due to their ability to simultaneously detect multiple wavelengths [300]. In contrast, narrow emissive CQDs are synthesized with a more uniform size distribution, resulting in a narrower emission spectrum with a distinct peak [301,302]. This spectral precision is usually attributed to similar-sized CQDs emitting light at specific wavelengths. These CQDs are ideal for high-definition displays requiring precise color control, laser technologies necessitating monochromatic light sources, and bioimaging applications where enhanced resolution and detection sensitivity are paramount [301]. While both types can be utilized in lighting applications, their strengths lie in different areas as highlighted previously [303].

Owing to their outstanding light-harvesting capabilities and conducting properties enabling CQDs to efficiently capture photons and generate excitons, which are then separated into electrons and holes for widespread application in various solar cell technologies [304–308]. CQDs can act as efficient sensitizers in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) owing to their broad absorption spectrum, high extinction coefficient, and tunable bandgap [308–311]. Despite the numerous advantages, CQD-sensitized DSSCs still exhibit lower efficiency than conventional DSSCs using ruthenium-based dyes. This is primarily attributed to photo-charged surface recombination, which diminishes current density by hindering charge carriers' effective separation and transport [304]. On the other hand, the CQDs can also be combined with other sensitizers, such as organic dyes or metal complexes, to form co-sensitized systems. This approach can broaden the absorption range of the DSSC, improve charge separation and injection efficiency, and reduce recombination losses, thereby enhancing the overall power conversion efficiency [312–315]. Subsequent investigations have corroborated the suitability of CQDs for photovoltaic applications, demonstrating their appropriate band alignment and efficient charge separation/electron injection capabilities [316–321]. This versatility extends beyond sensitized and co-sensitized DSSCs, proving effective in diverse device architectures, including bulk heterojunction (BHJ) and solid-state devices [304,305,317,318,322–329]. As we know, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of a solar cell, a measure of its effectiveness in transforming sunlight into electricity, is determined under standardized conditions (typically a solar spectrum of 100 mW/cm2 intensity). PCE is calculated as the ratio of the maximum power output (Pmax) to the input power from sunlight (Pin), expressed as a percentage. Notably, Pmax can be represented as the product of three key factors: open-circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current density (Jsc), and the fill factor (FF). Representative results on the CQDs-based solar cells devices performance are summarized in Table 3 .

In this context, Riaz et al. [288] reported solar cells performance of N-doped, S-doped and N,S-doped CQDs as sensitizers, with a PCE of 1.36%, VOC of 0.43 V, JSC 0.61 mA/cm−2 and FF of about 52%. At the same time, the utilization of CQDs such as active layers do not report interesting PEC values. CQDs are also used in the organic solar cells as active layer [318,345]. A significant hurdle to the commercial viability of CQD-based solar cells is their relatively lower PCE compared to traditional silicon-based counterparts. This disparity is primarily attributed to the high density of trap states within CQDs, which facilitate charge recombination and energy losses. Furthermore, ensuring the long-term stability of these devices poses another challenge, as CQDs are prone to degradation upon prolonged exposure to light and heat. However, the high absorption coefficient of CQDs allows for the use of thin active layers, reducing material costs and facilitating the fabrication of flexible devices.

As we know, the most widely used material in organic solar cells is a blend of poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) and the fullerene derivative (6,6)-phenyl-C61 butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM) [346]. To enhance the photovoltaic performance of these devices, CQDs have emerged as a promising third component in the photoactive blend layer. Hence, it is well-known that the incorporation of CQDs has been shown to improve multiple aspects of device functionality, including light absorption, exciton dissociation, and charge transport [347]. For instance, Kurukavak et al. [328] investigated the impact of incorporating blue-emitting phosphorus-doped CQDs at varying concentrations (0, 1, 3, 5, and 7 vol%) into the P3HT:PCBM active layer of organic solar cells. Notably, the authors found that a 1 vol% concentration of phosphorus-doped CQDs enhanced photon harvesting in the UV region and emitted visible light, resulting in an increase in PCE from 2.19% to 2.48%. The champion device achieved a FF of about 55.34%. Xu et al. [330] reported an interesting way to introduce CQDs on a perovskite matrix to improve its properties. As a result, the PEC values up to 13.28 % compared with 10.50% achieved without CQDs. Furthermore, the stability of these solar cells improved significantly, that is, suggesting that CQDs act as a protection of the perovskite layer in relative humidity of 40–45%. Generally, it is well-known that the quality of its perovskite layer significantly influences the performance and stability of a photovoltaic device. It has also been observed that incorporating CQDs contributes to an increase in the crystallinity of the perovskite layer, that is, contributing to an improvement of the device [332]. On other the hand, many studies have generally used CQDs as electron transport layer (ETL) or hole transport layer (HTL) in perovskite-based solar cells [344,348,349]. However, it has been observed that perovskite-based solar cells employing pure CQDs as ETLs or HTLs on different architectures exhibit lower PCEs [349].

For instance, Kim et al. [344] reported the incorporation effects of CQDs on nickel oxide (NiOx) layers as the HTL in the inverted perovskite-based solar cells performance. The PEC reach up to 17.02% with the configuration of NiOx:CQDs (95:5 wt%). In general, as is well-known, this improvement in the efficient transport of loads increased the crystallinity of devices prepared by adding CQDs. Hui et al. [350] demonstrated a significant enhancement in PCE from 19.15% to 22.77% in perovskite solar cells by incorporating red-light-emitting CQDs into the SnO2 ETL. Liu et al. [351] successfully improved the performance of orthotropic planar heterojunction perovskite solar cells by incorporating CQDs into the spiro-MeOTAD as HTL. The addition of CQDs deepened the valence band maximum of the HTL, leading to improved hole extraction efficiency. This, along with enhanced carrier collection and a more compact interface between the HTL and active layer, resulted in notable increases in open-circuit voltage, short-circuit current, and fill factor. As a result, the power conversion efficiency of the optimized devices reached 20.41%, a significant improvement over the initial 19.05%. Moreover, the modified HTL showed reduced defects and suppressed lithium-ion migration, contributing to enhanced device stability [351]. Notably, incorporating CQDs as dopants in HTLs, particularly in n-i-p architectures using spiro-OMeTAD, has been shown to significantly enhance device performance [349]. This improvement is usually attributed to the tunable bandgap of CQDs, which enables optimized energy level alignment between the HTL and perovskite layer, promoting efficient charge transfer process in device. Moreover, the presence of CQDs' surface functional groups play a crucial role in passivating defects both within the HTL and at interfaces, contributing to enhanced device performance [349].

From this perspective, theoretical models have been widely developed to simulate the performance of CQD-based devices, such as solar cells and LEDs [138,349,352–356]. These models take into account various factors, such as light absorption, charge generation, transport, and recombination, to predict the efficiency and performance of these devices under different operating conditions. Hence, the CQDs have emerged as a promising and sustainable alternative to traditional materials, and has the potential to game-changers in optoelectronic technology.

CQDs-based materials have attracted more and more attention due to their manufacturing simplicity as well as their enormous potential technological. This review provides a comprehensive understanding of the physical and chemical properties of CQDs-based materials are strongly related to its size and surface defects, as well as, which are strong dependence on the manufacturing method. Different synthesis strategies and precursors were discussed for preparing CQDs. While significant progress has been made in developing CQDs, several challenges remain to unlock their full potential. The diverse range of precursors and synthesis methods often results in heterogeneity, making it difficult to reproduce desired properties consistently. Additionally, many current synthesis techniques are limited to laboratory scales, hindering their mass production and widespread application [287].

To fully realize the potential of CQDs, future research should focus on developing scalable and cost-effective synthesis methods that ensure consistent quality and properties. Furthermore, it is essential to understand the intricate relationship between CQD structures (size, shape, surface chemistry) and their functional properties. However, the exact mechanisms governing light conversion in CQD-based optoelectronic devices, along with the precise structural and light emission details of CQDs, remain elusive. The current lack of clarity hinders the optimization of device design and the attainment of high-performance electroluminescent LEDs and solar cells [359]. By unlocking these relationships, we can engineer CQDs with tailored properties, opening doors to innovative applications across various fields. Ongoing studies have focused on understanding the electronic structure of the CQDs-like materials. Of course, computational design methods are at the forefront of knowledge and have a pivotal role in elucidating the electronic structure of several advanced materials, i.e., contributing to accelerating the development of better materials. Also, these studies can significantly contribute to new chemical insights towards materials design with highly tailoring properties. This fact, together with scalable synthesis, may in principle be highly beneficial to developing new emergent CQDs-based technologies. Additionally, we envision a future where CQDs play a pivotal role in addressing global challenges, such as clean energy generation, environmental remediation, disease diagnosis and treatment, and the development of advanced technological devices.
